output.diagnosis: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema secondary to advanced mitral valve disease
initial_stabilization: Administer 100% oxygen via mask or CPAP; monitor SpO2 and respiratory effort
Establish IV access; begin fluid restriction (0.5–1 mL/kg/h) to avoid volume overload
Give furosemide 1 mg/kg IV q6h (adjust based on urine output and electrolytes)
Start pimobendan 0.25 mg/kg PO q12h if not already on it, titrate up to 0.5 mg/kg q12h
Consider low‑dose ACE inhibitor (e.g., enalapril 0.1–0.2 mg/kg PO q12h) if not at therapeutic dose
diagnostic_workup: Thoracic radiographs: look for interstitial/subpleural edema, cardiomegaly
Point‑of‑care cardiac ultrasound (echocardiography): assess mitral regurgitation severity, left atrial size, pulmonary artery pressures
Arterial blood gas and lactate: evaluate hypoxemia, acid–base status
monitoring: Continuous heart rate, respiratory rate, SpO2, capillary refill time
Urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h; monitor electrolytes (Na, K, Cl) q6h
Repeat radiographs or ultrasound if clinical status changes
advanced_interventions: If dyspnea worsens despite CPAP: prepare for endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation
Consider diuretic escalation or vasodilators (e.g., nitroprusside) in refractory cases
Discuss long‑term options: surgical valve repair/replacement if feasible, or palliative care
owner_communication: Explain acute condition, need for hospitalization and monitoring
Discuss prognosis based on severity of mitral disease and response to therapy
Provide instructions for home care: medication schedule, signs of deterioration
